children.value: Wing twist (washout) reduces the angle of attack at the tip relative to the root. This serves two purposes:
Prevents tip stall before root stall
, which is critical for maintaining aileron effectiveness and safe stall recovery.
Helps approximate an elliptical spanwise lift distribution
A typical washout angle for general aviation is

children.alt: Pitching moment contributions from each aircraft component. The tail provides a stabilizing (positive slope) contribution, while the fuselage and wing alone are destabilizing. The complete aircraft curve must have a negative slope (dM_{ac}/d\alpha < 0) for static stability. The trim condition occurs where M_{cg} = 0.

children.value: ). It establishes the forward and aft c.g. limits based on three criteria:
ensures sufficient nose-down control authority at stall.
ensures sufficient nose-up moment to rotate during take-off.
The intersection of these constraints determines the minimum required tail size.
when it does not rotate about the c.g. in flight. Two conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

children.value: Trim drag is the additional drag resulting from the deflection of the horizontal stabilizer and elevator to maintain the trim condition. Since the tail typically produces a downward force, the wing must produce extra lift to compensate, resulting in a slight increase in induced drag:
C_D = C_{D_0} + \frac{C_{L_w}^2}{\pi \cdot e_w \cdot AR_w} + \frac{C_{L_t}^2}{\pi \cdot e_t \cdot AR_t} \cdot \frac{S_t}{S_w}
children.label: eq:trim-drag
